claims.claim_text: The opening incident describes the 2019 Capital One breach as exposing over 100 million records after a misconfigured WAF was abused to reach AWS metadata services and obtain credentials.
claims.span_hint: Why This Module Matters, first paragraph
claims.rationale: The incident is real and appears supportable from DOJ case materials, but justice.gov is off the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The module claims that default-deny segmentation would have isolated the breach to a single container and completely contained it.
claims.span_hint: Why This Module Matters, first paragraph
claims.anchor_text: If a robust, default-deny network segmentation strategy had been enforced, the incident would have been isolated to a single container and completely contained.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A robust default-deny network segmentation strategy can limit lateral movement and reduce the blast radius of a compromise.
claims.rationale: This is an unsupported counterfactual with absolute certainty rather than a directly citable fact.

claims.claim_text: In a default Kubernetes namespace with no NetworkPolicies, pods can communicate without NetworkPolicy-based ingress or egress restrictions.
claims.span_hint: Why This Module Matters, second paragraph; Part 2 opening
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes Network Policies concept page directly states that without policies, ingress and egress are allowed.

claims.claim_text: The module states that the CKA exam aggressively tests NetworkPolicy authoring and troubleshooting and will require these exact tasks.
claims.span_hint: Why This Module Matters, paragraph after analogy
claims.anchor_text: Because of this monumental security impact, the Certified Kubernetes Administrator (CKA) exam aggressively tests your ability to rapidly author, evaluate, and troubleshoot Network Policies under pressure. You will be required to build surgical ingress and egress rules, trace traffic drops to missing namespace labels, and securely orchestrate communication between complex multi-tier applications.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Because network isolation is an important Kubernetes administration skill, you should be able to rapidly author, evaluate, and troubleshoot NetworkPolicies under pressure.
claims.rationale: The official curriculum does not clearly back this exam-specific certainty and task list as written.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes currently maintains active release branches for 1.35, 1.34, and 1.33, and Kubernetes 1.35 reaches end of life on February 28, 2027.
claims.span_hint: Did You Know, first bullet; Part 5 closing sentence
claims.rationale: The releases page directly lists the active branches and the 1.35 end-of-life date.

claims.claim_text: The stable NetworkPolicy API is networking.k8s.io/v1, available since v1.8, and extensions/v1beta1 stopped being served in v1.16.
claims.span_hint: Did You Know, first bullet; Quiz 6 answer
claims.rationale: The deprecation guide explicitly gives the removal version and the replacement API version and availability.

claims.claim_text: The endPort field for NetworkPolicy port ranges is stable as of Kubernetes v1.25.
claims.span_hint: Did You Know, second bullet; Part 5 endPort paragraph
claims.rationale: The Network Policies page marks endPort as feature state Kubernetes v1.25 [stable].

claims.claim_text: The module claims that nearly 80% of production NetworkPolicy debugging sessions stem from missing DNS egress on port 53.
claims.anchor_text: Nearly 80% of all NetworkPolicy debugging sessions in production environments ultimately stem from a missing DNS egress rule on port 53, which silently breaks internal service discovery the moment a default-deny egress policy is deployed.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A common NetworkPolicy debugging issue is forgetting DNS egress on port 53, which breaks service discovery when default-deny egress is deployed.
claims.rationale: The exact percentage is not backed by an authoritative source in the allowlist and should be removed.

claims.claim_text: NetworkPolicy is defined for layer 4 traffic and does not provide layer 7 controls such as HTTP-path or TLS/SNI filtering.
claims.span_hint: Did You Know, fourth bullet; Part 5 limitations
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes docs state NetworkPolicy is for layer 4 traffic and list TLS- and service-name-related gaps.

claims.claim_text: An empty root podSelector ({}) selects all pods in the namespace, and using it with Ingress and/or Egress default-deny patterns isolates all pods for that direction.
claims.rationale: The default deny examples and podSelector documentation directly describe this behavior.

claims.claim_text: Using an empty rule object ({}) inside ingress or egress explicitly allows all traffic for that direction.
claims.rationale: The allow-all ingress and allow-all egress examples show ingress: [{}] and egress: [{}] semantics directly.

claims.claim_text: If policyTypes is omitted, Ingress is always implied, and Egress is only implied when egress rules are present.
claims.rationale: The resource description explicitly states these defaulting rules for policyTypes.

claims.claim_text: NetworkPolicies are additive and order-independent, and pod-to-pod traffic is allowed only if the source egress policy and destination ingress policy both allow it.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes docs directly state that policies are additive, order does not matter, and both sides must allow a connection.

claims.claim_text: A single from/to entry combining namespaceSelector and podSelector is an AND, while separate array entries are OR.
claims.rationale: The selector-behavior section shows both YAML forms and explains the difference explicitly.

claims.claim_text: ipBlock is intended for cluster-external CIDRs, and source or destination IP rewriting can make NetworkPolicy matching against original versus rewritten IPs undefined.
claims.rationale: The Network Policies page directly discusses ipBlock scope and the ambiguity introduced by packet rewriting.

claims.claim_text: The API rejects ipBlock except CIDRs that fall outside the parent cidr range.
claims.rationale: The generated API docs state this validation rule directly, but pkg.go.dev is off the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: A default deny-all egress policy also blocks DNS traffic, so workloads that need DNS resolution require an explicit egress allow to cluster DNS.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes docs explicitly warn that deny-all egress blocks DNS and requires a separate allow policy.

claims.claim_text: Creating a NetworkPolicy has no effect unless the cluster's network plugin implements NetworkPolicy enforcement.
claims.span_hint: Part 5 opening; Debugging Workflow; Quiz 1 answer
claims.rationale: The docs explicitly state that NetworkPolicy resources do nothing without a supporting network plugin.

claims.claim_text: Basic Flannel is focused on networking and does not itself provide NetworkPolicy enforcement; other projects such as Calico are used for that.
claims.span_hint: Part 5 opening; Common Mistakes; Quiz 1 answer
claims.rationale: The Flannel README says Flannel is focused on networking and points users to other projects for network policy.

claims.claim_text: NetworkPolicy handling is asynchronous, newly affected pods may briefly start with different connectivity than expected, and existing connections on policy changes are implementation-defined.
claims.rationale: The pod lifecycle and existing-connections sections describe delayed handling and implementation-defined behavior directly.

claims.claim_text: If a network plugin does not support endPort, a policy using endPort is applied only for the single starting port.
claims.rationale: The endPort note explicitly describes this fallback behavior for unsupported plugins.

claims.claim_text: NetworkPolicy behavior for hostNetwork pods is undefined, and the most common implementation treats their traffic as node traffic rather than normal pod traffic.
claims.rationale: The hostNetwork section says behavior is undefined and identifies node-traffic treatment as the most common implementation.

claims.claim_text: As of Kubernetes 1.35, the NetworkPolicy API still cannot express explicit deny policies, service-name targeting, node-identity targeting, TLS-related controls, or loopback blocking.
claims.rationale: The limitations section on the official docs lists these missing capabilities for Kubernetes 1.35.

further_reading.title: Network Policies
further_reading.why_relevant: Canonical reference for isolation rules, selector semantics, limitations, DNS caveats, and hostNetwork behavior.

further_reading.title: Declare Network Policy
further_reading.why_relevant: Provides worked examples for authoring default-deny and selective allow policies.

further_reading.title: DNS for Services and Pods
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains why service-name access depends on cluster DNS and why DNS egress matters when restricting traffic.

notes: Focused on prose and quiz-answer prose, not code blocks, Mermaid diagrams, or generic exercise steps. Repeated claims were deduplicated where the same citation can support multiple spans.
